text: Hello, this is Dr. Jimmy Hsu and this is my video series on the use of the robotic platform on emergent cases. This is a case of a perforated diverticulitis.

I want to start out just talking a little bit about port placement. I essentially place my ports in a diagonal line from the left upper quadrant to the right lower quadrant. And I have a planned space for our ostomy. However, most of the time in these cases, we can't have stoma markings or anything like that.

One of the things that might be a little strange is that I only place three ports. I don't usually plan on placing three ports. However, having a really good first assist at the bedside is almost as good as having that fourth robotic arm and gives you the freedom and mobility to kind of adjust arm placement as you need a lot of times i will use a robotic suction irrigator for cases like this however the first assist i had this day i knew was very competent and could essentially do the job of that suction irrigator and understand the details of this case.

I am careful in the patient selections I utilize for these robotic platform sigmoidectomies and Hartman procedures as I don't do significant perforations or gross contamination. But I have found that patients overall have a shorter length of stay, reduced incisional hernia rate, and it makes the takedown operation in the future much easier.

I knew on the CT scan of this lady that she actually had two abscesses and you're about to see us enter the first one right now. Later on you'll see where we find the second abscess to enter it as well.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection using robotic instruments, mimicking hand movements typical of open surgery, to navigate the inflamed tissue associated with perforated diverticulitis.
The surgeon uses a black load stapler to transect the rectum, opting not to dissect further due to significant inflammation visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to enter the second abscess identified on the CT scan, visibly draining its contents.
The surgeon tags the rectal stump using sutures, preparing it for identification in a future takedown operation. The vessel sealer is utilized for suturing to minimize instrument costs.
The surgeon brings out the rectal stump and transects it at the skin level, preparing to create an ostomy after removing the diseased portion of the colon.
I hope you enjoyed this video and see what's possible with the robotic platform. I'm hoping to shoot a series of these kinds of videos in the future.
